A firm belief in the doctrine of God's universal providence, and its extent, would satisfy us when in danger and encourage us to trust God in doing what is right. Providence notices even the smallest creatures, like sparrows, and therefore cares for the smallest interests of Christ’s followers. Those who confess Christ now will be recognized by him on that great day before God's angels. To discourage us from denying Christ and abandoning his truths and ways, we are assured that those who deny Christ, even if they save their own lives or gain a kingdom through it, will ultimately suffer great loss; for Christ will not know them, recognize them, or favor them. But let no backslider who feels remorse doubt that he can be forgiven. This is different from determined hatred against the Holy Spirit, which cannot be forgiven because it will never be repented of.

Christ’s kingdom is spiritual and not part of this world. Christianity does not involve itself in politics; it requires everyone to act justly, but worldly power is not based on grace. It does not encourage expectations of material advantages through religion. The rewards for Christ's followers are of a different kind. Greed is a sin we must constantly be warned against; because happiness and comfort do not depend on the wealth of this world. Material things will not satisfy a person’s desires. Here is a story that shows how foolish people who focus on worldly matters are while they live, and their misery when they die. The character described is exactly like a practical, worldly man who has no gratitude for God's care or any proper understanding of the uncertainty of life, the value of his soul, or the importance of eternity. How many, even among those who claim to be Christians, point to people like him as examples and suitable companions! We are mistaken if we think that thoughts can be hidden or are free. When he saw a large harvest on his land, instead of thanking God for it or rejoicing in being able to do more good, he worried about what to do next. The poorest beggar could not have expressed a more anxious thought. The more people have, the more trouble they have with it. It was foolish for him to think of using his wealth only to indulge himself and satisfy his desires without helping others. People who focus on worldly matters are fools; and the day is coming when God will call them by name, and they will acknowledge that themselves. The death of such people is miserable in itself and terrifying to them. Your soul will be required. He does not want to give it up; but God will require it, demand an explanation for it, demand it as a guilty soul to be punished without delay. It is the mistake of most people to focus on what benefits their bodies and lasts only for a short time, more than on what benefits their souls and lasts forever.

Christ strongly emphasized this caution against becoming anxious and troubled. The arguments here are meant to encourage us to entrust our cares to God, which is the right way to find relief. Just as it is wise to grow in stature, so it is wise to accept things as they are. An eager, anxious pursuit of worldly possessions, even necessities, does not become Christ’s followers. We must not be afraid; when we frighten ourselves with thoughts of future troubles and worry about how to avoid them, we create unnecessary burdens for ourselves. If we value holiness, we will not crave luxury. Let us then examine whether we belong to this small group. Christ is our Master, and we are his servants; not only working servants but also waiting servants. We must be like people who wait for their lord, staying awake while he is out late, ready to welcome him back. In this, Christ was referring to his own ascension into heaven, his coming to call his people to himself through death, and his return to judge the world. Since we do not know when he will come to us, we should always be prepared. If people take care of their homes in this way, let us be equally wise concerning our souls. Therefore, you too must be ready; as ready as the homeowner would be if he knew when a thief was coming.

Everyone should embrace what Christ says in his word and seek to understand it. No one is so ignorant that they do not know many things are wrong and many things are right which they neglect; therefore, everyone is without excuse for their sin. Introducing the gospel would bring about destruction. Not because Christ’s religion itself tends toward destruction—it is pure, peaceful, and loving—but because it opposes people's pride and desires. There was to be a widespread spreading of the good news. But before that could happen, Christ had a baptism to undergo, different from one with water and the Holy Spirit. He must suffer and die. It did not fit his plan to spread the gospel more widely until this baptism was completed. We should be eager to share the truth, for even though divisions will arise and family members may become enemies, sinners will be converted, and God will be glorified.

Christ wanted people to be as wise in matters concerning their souls as they are in practical affairs. Let them hurry to make peace with God before it is too late. If anyone has experienced God’s opposition because of his sins, let him turn to God through Christ, who reconciles the world to himself. While we are alive, we are on our way, and now is our time.
